subject: ap-government
unit_name: Political Parties, Interest Groups, and Elections

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The constitutional amendment (1964) that prohibited poll taxes in federal elections, removing a financial barrier to voting that had disenfranchised many minority and low-income citizens

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The constitutional amendment (1971) that lowered the voting age from 21 to 18, extending suffrage to younger Americans in the context of the Vietnam War

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The percentage of eligible voters who actually cast ballots in a given election; consistently lower in the U.S. than in most other democracies

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A citizen's belief that their vote and political participation actually matter and can influence government outcomes; higher levels correlate with higher voter turnout

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A model of voting behavior in which citizens weigh the costs and benefits of participating and choose candidates who best serve their self-interest

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Voting based on an evaluation of an incumbent's past performance — rewarding or punishing elected officials for what they have already done in office

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Voting based on a candidate's promises and policy positions for the future — choosing the candidate whose platform aligns with what a voter wants government to do going forward

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The practice of voting for every candidate of a single political party on the ballot, regardless of individual candidates' qualifications

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Organizations that connect citizens to government by translating citizen preferences into government policy; includes political parties, interest groups, elections, and the media

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A group of people organized to influence government by nominating candidates for office and winning elections in order to control the policymaking process

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Voting for candidates from different parties for different offices in the same election — the opposite of straight-ticket voting

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A written statement of a political party's core beliefs, principles, and policy positions, adopted at the national convention and used to signal the party's direction to voters

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An election in which voters select a party's candidate who will then compete in the general election; the mechanism through which parties narrow the field of nominees

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A primary election in which any registered voter may participate, regardless of party affiliation — voters do not need to be registered members of the party whose primary they vote in

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A primary election restricted to voters who are registered members of a particular party; only Democrats can vote in the Democratic primary, only Republicans in the Republican primary

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An electoral system in which legislative seats are allocated to parties based on their share of the total vote, allowing smaller parties to win representation; used in many European democracies but not for U.S. federal elections

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An electoral system in which one representative is chosen per geographic district by plurality vote; the basis of the U.S. House of Representatives and a key structural reason for the two-party system

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Interest groups organized around a particular political philosophy or set of values (e.g., conservative, liberal, libertarian) rather than a single economic interest

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Interest groups that claim to represent broad societal benefits rather than narrow economic or professional interests; examples include consumer advocacy and environmental organizations

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Interest groups that concentrate entirely on one specific policy issue, such as gun control or abortion, and refuse to compromise on that issue to build broader coalitions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Organizations that represent people who share the same occupation or profession — such as the American Medical Association or the American Bar Association — and lobby on behalf of their members' interests

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The practice of attempting to influence government officials — especially legislators — on behalf of a group or cause; protected by the First Amendment's right to petition the government

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The stable, mutually beneficial relationship among a congressional committee, a federal agency, and an interest group that all share a common policy interest and reinforce each other's power

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A loosely connected network of interest groups, government officials, experts, and journalists who all care about a policy area and interact informally — more open and less stable than an iron triangle

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A form of lobbying that mobilizes the general public or a group's members to contact elected officials directly, creating the appearance of widespread citizen pressure on a policy issue

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A federally regulated fundraising committee established by a corporation, union, or interest group to collect and donate money to political campaigns within legal limits

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An electoral rule in which the candidate who wins the most votes in a district receives all of that district's electoral votes or seats, with no reward for second place — the basis of the U.S. Electoral College and congressional elections

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A state where neither major political party has a consistent majority and the outcome is genuinely competitive; presidential campaigns concentrate advertising and resources heavily in these states

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Any action taken by citizens to influence government — including voting, contacting officials, donating money, protesting, and running for office

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A large, informal network of people and organizations working outside established institutions to bring about broad social or political change, often through protest and civil disobedience

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The legal right to vote; also called suffrage — historically expanded by constitutional amendments to include Black men (15th), women (19th), and 18-year-olds (26th)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A fee required to be paid before a citizen could register to vote; historically used in Southern states to disenfranchise Black voters and the poor; banned in federal elections by the Twenty-Fourth Amendment (1964)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Statistical traits of a population used to explain voting behavior, including age, race, gender, education level, income, and religion

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A composite measure of an individual's economic and social position based on income, education, and occupational prestige; one of the strongest predictors of voter turnout

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Efforts by political parties, campaigns, and interest groups to encourage citizens to vote or become politically active through outreach, registration drives, and canvassing

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: State and local rules requiring citizens to sign up on an official list before they can vote; critics argue these rules reduce turnout by creating an additional barrier to participation

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A ballot cast by a voter who cannot appear at their polling place on Election Day, submitted in advance by mail or in person before the election

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The body of 538 electors — allocated to states based on congressional representation — who formally elect the President; a candidate needs 270 electoral votes to win

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A closely contested state in a presidential election where either major party candidate could win; presidential campaigns spend disproportionate resources here compared to reliably partisan states

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Organized efforts by parties, campaigns, and interest groups to increase voter turnout among their supporters through phone banking, door-to-door canvassing, and ride-to-polls programs

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The broad grouping of demographic and interest groups that consistently support a particular political party — for example, the Democratic coalition has historically included labor unions, Black voters, and urban professionals

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A major, lasting shift in the partisan loyalties of voters and party coalitions, producing a new majority party that dominates elections for a generation; typically triggered by a critical election

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An election that signals a fundamental, durable shift in party coalitions and the balance of power between parties — such as the 1932 election that established the New Deal Democratic majority

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A period of American political history dominated by one political party, lasting from one critical election or realignment to the next

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A period in which one party controls the presidency while the other controls one or both chambers of Congress, making unified policy action difficult; increasingly common since the 1970s

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The official selection of a political party's candidate for office through the primary or caucus process; winners of this process advance to compete in the general election

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A party member selected at the state or district level to attend the national convention and cast a vote for a presidential nominee on behalf of primary/caucus voters

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A local party meeting in which registered voters publicly discuss and vote for their preferred presidential candidate, used by Iowa and a small number of other states in the nominating process

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: In the Democratic Party, an unpledged delegate who is a party leader or elected official and may vote for any candidate at the national convention regardless of primary results

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The tendency of states to schedule their primaries and caucuses earlier in the election calendar to gain greater influence over the presidential nomination process, compressing the nominating season

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The formal gathering of a political party's delegates, held every four years, to officially nominate the presidential and vice-presidential candidates and adopt the party platform

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A style of electoral politics in which individual candidates build their own fundraising, media, and organizing operations independently from party infrastructure, relying on personal image over party loyalty

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A political system dominated by two major parties; structural features like single-member districts, winner-take-all rules, and ballot access laws make it extremely difficult for third parties to compete effectively in the United States

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Any political party other than the two dominant parties; in U.S. politics, these parties face structural barriers like winner-take-all elections and ballot access requirements, making electoral success rare

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Regulated campaign donations given directly to a candidate or party committee, subject to Federal Election Commission contribution limits and disclosure requirements

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Unregulated campaign contributions donated to political parties for 'party-building' activities — largely restricted by the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act of 2002 (McCain-Feingold)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The network of voluntary associations, non-profits, civic groups, and other organizations that exist between the individual and the state; seen as essential to healthy democratic participation

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The theory that political power is widely distributed among many competing interest groups, each with some influence, and that no single group dominates — government acts as a referee balancing competing interests

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The theory that a small, wealthy upper class dominates political decision-making regardless of democratic elections, using its economic resources and social networks to shape policy outcomes

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The set of issues that the government and public treat as priorities at any given time; interest groups, the media, and elected officials all compete to influence which problems receive attention

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The challenge of organizing people to contribute to a shared goal when each individual has an incentive to let others do the work and enjoy the benefit anyway; the central problem facing interest groups

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A benefit that, once provided, is available to all members of a group regardless of whether they contributed to obtaining it — examples include clean air regulations or national defense

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Individuals who benefit from a collective good without contributing to its production, knowing they cannot be excluded from the benefit; their existence makes organizing large groups difficult

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Exclusive advantages — such as discounts, publications, or networking events — offered only to dues-paying members of an interest group to solve the free rider problem and encourage membership

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Interest groups that represent business, labor, and professional sectors — the largest and best-funded category, including corporations, trade associations, and labor unions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Interest groups composed of governments or government agencies that lobby other levels of government — such as the National Governors Association advocating before Congress

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The movement of individuals between government regulatory positions and the private industries they once regulated — critics argue this creates conflicts of interest that benefit industry over the public

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A written legal argument submitted to a court by a party who is not directly involved in a case but has an interest in its outcome; interest groups frequently file these to influence Supreme Court decisions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A form of political participation in which citizens publicly demonstrate their opposition to a policy or government action through marches, rallies, picketing, or demonstrations

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The deliberate, nonviolent refusal to obey unjust laws as a form of political protest; practitioners openly accept legal punishment to expose the injustice and build public moral pressure for change

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 2010 Supreme Court decision that struck down limits on independent political expenditures by corporations and unions, ruling that political spending is a form of protected speech under the First Amendment

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A veterans' organization lobbies Congress to increase defense spending. The Department of Defense supports the increase. A Senate Armed Services subcommittee approves the expanded budget. Which concept best describes this relationship?

choices.text: Iron Triangle, because a congressional committee, federal agency, and interest group mutually reinforce each other's policy goals
choices.explanation: The iron triangle is the stable three-way relationship among a congressional committee (Senate Armed Services), a federal agency (Department of Defense), and an interest group (veterans' organization) — each benefits the others and together they dominate the policy area.

choices.text: Issue Network, because multiple actors are loosely coordinating on a shared policy concern
choices.explanation: Issue networks are looser, more fluid coalitions with many actors including academics, journalists, and outside advocates — the iron triangle specifically describes the tight three-way relationship between committee, agency, and interest group.

choices.text: Pluralist Theory, because competing groups are all able to influence defense spending decisions
choices.explanation: Pluralism describes a system of many competing groups — the scenario describes a closed, mutually reinforcing relationship among just three specific actors, which is the definition of an iron triangle, not broad pluralist competition.

choices.text: Grassroots Lobbying, because the organization is pressuring Congress on behalf of veterans
choices.explanation: Grassroots lobbying mobilizes ordinary citizens to contact officials — direct lobbying of a committee by an organized group, combined with agency support, describes the iron triangle structure, not a grassroots pressure campaign.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A presidential candidate wins the popular vote nationally but loses the Electoral College. Which structural feature of the Electoral College most directly explains this outcome?

choices.text: The winner-take-all system used by most states, which awards all electoral votes to the plurality winner regardless of margin
choices.explanation: Because most states use winner-take-all allocation, a candidate can run up large margins in a few states while narrowly losing many others — winning more total votes but fewer electoral votes than the opponent.

choices.text: Front-loading, which gives early primary states disproportionate influence over the general election
choices.explanation: Front-loading affects the nominating process, not the general election — it does not determine how electoral votes are allocated between the two major-party nominees.

choices.text: The proportional representation system used by most states
choices.explanation: Only Maine and Nebraska use proportional allocation — the vast majority of states use winner-take-all, which is precisely why a popular vote winner can lose the Electoral College.

choices.text: Swing states receiving fewer electoral votes than their population would warrant under pure proportional allocation
choices.explanation: Swing states do not receive fewer votes than warranted — the popular/electoral vote split is driven by winner-take-all allocation across states of varying size, not by swing states being underrepresented.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A political scientist argues that wealthy corporations and their lobbyists dominate Washington policymaking and that ordinary citizens have little real influence. A critic responds that many competing groups — labor unions, environmental organizations, and consumer advocates — also shape policy. The critic is invoking which theory?

choices.text: Elitist Theory, because the critic is emphasizing that organized groups, not individuals, hold power
choices.explanation: Elitist Theory holds that a small, wealthy upper class dominates — the critic's argument that many different groups have influence is exactly the opposite position, which is pluralism.

choices.text: Pluralist Theory, because the critic argues that power is dispersed among many competing interest groups
choices.explanation: Pluralism holds that political power is distributed broadly among many competing groups and that government serves as a referee balancing their competing interests — the critic's claim that labor, environmental, and consumer groups also shape policy is the classic pluralist rebuttal to elite domination.

choices.text: Collective Action Theory, because the critic is pointing out that groups overcome free rider problems
choices.explanation: Collective action theory addresses the problem of organizing groups — not whether power is concentrated or dispersed across society. The debate between the political scientist and critic is about the distribution of political power, which is the pluralism vs. elitism debate.

choices.text: Realignment Theory, because the critic is suggesting a shift in which groups hold power over time
choices.explanation: Realignment refers to long-term shifts in party coalitions — not to who holds political power at any given time. The critic's argument about competing group influence is pluralism, not realignment.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An environmental group advocates for clean air regulations that would benefit all Americans. Despite broad public support, the group struggles to recruit paying members because people know they will benefit from the regulations even if they do not join. This scenario best illustrates which concept?

choices.text: The Free Rider Problem — individuals benefit from collective goods without contributing to their production
choices.explanation: Clean air is a collective good — once achieved, everyone benefits whether or not they paid dues. Knowing they cannot be excluded from the benefit, rational individuals have less incentive to join, creating the free rider problem that undermines collective action.

choices.text: Grassroots Lobbying — the group is failing to mobilize ordinary citizens effectively
choices.explanation: Grassroots lobbying is a tactic for pressuring officials — the scenario describes a membership recruitment problem caused by non-excludable benefits, which is specifically the free rider problem.

choices.text: The Revolving Door — former regulators are undermining the group's ability to gain new members
choices.explanation: The revolving door describes the movement of individuals between government and industry — it has no bearing on why people decline to pay dues to an environmental group when they will benefit regardless.

choices.text: Selective Benefits — the group is failing to offer exclusive incentives to members
choices.explanation: Selective benefits are the solution to the free rider problem, not the problem itself — the scenario describes why groups struggle to recruit (free riding), and selective benefits are what groups offer to overcome this obstacle.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: In the 2020 election, a voter decided to support the incumbent president because unemployment had dropped and economic growth was strong during his term. This voter is best described as engaging in which type of voting behavior?

choices.text: Retrospective Voting — the voter is rewarding the incumbent based on past economic performance
choices.explanation: Retrospective voting involves evaluating candidates based on their past performance in office — a voter who supports the incumbent because the economy performed well during his term is retrospectively rewarding good outcomes.

choices.text: Prospective Voting — the voter is choosing the candidate most likely to improve the economy in the future
choices.explanation: Prospective voting is forward-looking — it involves evaluating candidates' future policy promises. This voter's decision is based on past outcomes, which is the defining feature of retrospective voting.

choices.text: Party-Line Voting — the voter is supporting the candidate of their registered party
choices.explanation: Party-line voting occurs when a voter supports every candidate of a single party — the scenario describes a specific performance-based calculation about the incumbent, not unconditional party loyalty.

choices.text: Rational Choice Voting — the voter is calculating expected utility from supporting each candidate
choices.explanation: While retrospective voting can be rational, rational choice voting broadly refers to cost-benefit analysis of participation — retrospective voting is the more specific and accurate term for evaluating a candidate based on past governing outcomes.

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: In Citizens United v. FEC (2010), the Supreme Court held that limits on independent political expenditures by corporations and unions were unconstitutional. The Court's reasoning most directly reflected which principle?

choices.text: Political spending is a form of protected speech under the First Amendment, and the government may not limit independent expenditures
choices.explanation: Citizens United held that corporations and unions have First Amendment speech rights, and that independent political spending (not coordinated with a campaign) is protected expression — government limits on such spending were struck down as unconstitutional content-based restrictions.

choices.text: The Seventeenth Amendment guarantees corporations the same voting rights as individual citizens
choices.explanation: The Seventeenth Amendment established direct election of senators — it has nothing to do with corporate political spending or First Amendment speech rights at issue in Citizens United.

choices.text: Hard money donations to candidates cannot be regulated because they are a form of political association
choices.explanation: Citizens United addressed independent expenditures — spending not coordinated with campaigns — not direct contributions (hard money). The Court has upheld limits on direct contributions as distinct from independent spending.

choices.text: The Equal Protection Clause requires that corporations receive the same campaign finance protections as individual voters
choices.explanation: Citizens United was decided on First Amendment free speech grounds, not Equal Protection grounds — the Court held that political spending is speech, not that corporations must be treated identically to voters under the Fourteenth Amendment.
